questions.question: Define the NIST cloud computing reference architecture and list the five major actors (building blocks) defined in NIST SP 500‑292.
keyPoints: Definition of NIST cloud reference architecture
Five actors: Cloud Consumer, Cloud Provider, Cloud Broker, Cloud Auditor, Cloud Carrier
Roles and responsibilities of each actor

questions.question: Compare the three cloud service models—IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS—with respect to the level of control, management responsibilities, and provide a real‑world example for each.
keyPoints: IaaS: provides virtualized compute/storage, e.g., Amazon EC2
PaaS: offers a development platform, e.g., Google App Engine
SaaS: delivers ready‑to‑use software, e.g., Microsoft Office 365
Control/management trade‑offs across the three models

questions.question: Discuss the four cloud deployment models—Public, Private, Community, and Hybrid—highlighting their advantages, security considerations, and include a case study of an organization that has adopted one of these models.
keyPoints: Public cloud: owned by third‑party provider, cost‑effective, scalable; example: NASA using AWS for data processing
Private cloud: dedicated to a single organization, higher control and security; example: hospital private cloud for PHI
Community cloud: shared by organisations with common requirements; example: government community cloud for defence
Hybrid cloud: combines public and private; example: retail hybrid cloud for e‑commerce and on‑premises inventory

questions.question: Analyze a real‑world case study (e.g., Netflix migration to AWS) using the NIST architectural framework. Identify the actors involved, the service model(s) used, the deployment model, and evaluate the benefits and challenges faced.
keyPoints: Case study: Netflix migration to AWS
Actors: Cloud Consumer (Netflix), Cloud Provider (AWS), Cloud Broker/Edge (CloudFront)
Service models: IaaS (EC2) and PaaS (Kinesis, Lambda) on public cloud
Benefits: elasticity, global reach, cost optimization; Challenges: data security, latency, vendor lock‑in
